title: CollabWrite
content:
CollabWrite is a real-time collaborative editing backend built to support low-latency multi-user document editing. It was built as a backend systems project focused on solving real-time synchronization, concurrent editing, and conflict resolution in shared documents. The stack used FastAPI, WebSockets, PostgreSQL, and Hugging Face models for AI-assisted text actions. This project was built as a production-style backend system, though not shipped as a public production product.

The backend uses FastAPI WebSockets and an in-memory connection manager to maintain long-lived document sessions, cursor state, and user presence. Instead of relying on stateless REST polling, active editing happens through persistent WebSocket connections that broadcast incremental document operations across connected users.

To support concurrent editing without locking the document, the backend uses a custom character-level CRDT. Each character is assigned a deterministic position using a site identifier, local counter, and timestamp, allowing concurrent inserts to resolve consistently across clients without central locking or Operational Transformation overhead.

A major challenge was preventing write amplification during high-frequency edits. Writing every keystroke directly to PostgreSQL would create lock contention and excessive write pressure, so live document state is held in memory and flushed periodically through an async persistence loop.

The system also had to handle stale WebSocket sessions, ghost cursors, and memory leaks caused by dropped client connections. This was solved using heartbeat-based session tracking, ping/ack liveness checks, and periodic async cleanup to evict inactive users and stabilize in-memory session state.

To support AI-assisted editing features like paraphrasing, model inference was moved off the main async event loop into a thread-pool executor so that transformer inference would not block active WebSocket sessions or freeze real-time collaboration.

title: DocuMind
content:
DocuMind is an agent-based document intelligence system built to answer questions over documents using retrieval, structured extraction, and citation-backed generation. It was built as an applied AI backend project focused on document QA, retrieval quality, and grounded LLM responses. The stack used FastAPI, LangGraph, LangChain, ChromaDB, Python, and local or hosted LLMs. This project was built as an applied AI backend system and production-style architecture, but not deployed as a customer-facing production product.

The system uses a stateful multi-step RAG pipeline built with LangGraph instead of a linear chain. Query execution is split into dedicated stages for routing, retrieval, extraction, and response generation so failures in one stage do not collapse the full pipeline.

A major challenge was balancing structured extraction with natural response generation. This was solved by splitting the workflow into separate extraction and analysis stages, where one step first extracts structured fields and a later step converts them into grounded narrative output.

The retrieval pipeline uses ChromaDB with metadata-aware chunking and embedding search to support filtered semantic retrieval and reliable source-level traceability. Each chunk stores structured metadata such as source, page, and chunk index so the final response remains auditable.

To improve retrieval quality on broad queries, the system uses query planning, multi-pass retrieval, and context deduplication before ranking. Complex questions are split into smaller search queries, and duplicate chunks are removed before prompt assembly to reduce context pollution and improve retrieval density.

Because smaller local models were not always reliable for structured routing, the system includes fallback heuristics when structured output parsing fails. This keeps the retrieval flow stable even when LLM-based routing breaks.

To reduce hallucinations, the system enforces source-aware prompting, citation guardrails, and grounded response constraints so outputs remain tied to retrieved document context instead of unconstrained generation.

title: E-Beat
content:
E-Beat is a geospatial operational backend built for law-enforcement workflow management and field coordination. The project originated after winning a Goa Police hackathon, where the team proposed a practical approach to modernizing operational workflows used by field officers and higher-level management. Following the win, the team was brought in to help build the actual platform. The stack used Django, PostgreSQL, PostGIS, Firebase, and AWS. This project was shipped into production and used in live operational environments by field teams.

The platform was designed to centralize operational workflows such as officer tracking, geospatial task assignment, reporting, and field coordination across multiple administrative levels.

The backend uses PostGIS to model hierarchical spatial boundaries such as districts, stations, and beat areas, enforcing strict parent-child spatial constraints across operational zones.

A major challenge was handling high-frequency live officer tracking without overwhelming the primary relational database. To solve this, the system used a hybrid architecture where PostgreSQL and PostGIS handled structured operational data while Firebase Realtime Database handled high-frequency telemetry for real-time location streaming.

The platform also included workflow-controlled data moderation using a staging-table approval pattern, where field-submitted spatial records were written to an intermediate state first and promoted into production tables only after administrative approval.

Because field officers often operated in low-bandwidth environments, image uploads were automatically compressed server-side using signal-driven image optimization to reduce payload size and improve sync reliability over slower mobile networks.

The system also enforced spatial workflow constraints, ensuring operational tasks could only be assigned within valid jurisdictional boundaries defined in the geospatial model.

title: Workforce and Event Management Platform
content:
This was a freelance backend project built as a cloud-based workforce and event management platform for an operations-driven business that needed a centralized system for workforce coordination, attendance, event execution, and reporting. The project came in as a freelance engagement to replace fragmented operational workflows with a more scalable cloud-hosted backend. The stack used Django, PostgreSQL, AWS, Celery, Redis, S3, and Django Channels. This project was shipped into production and used in live business operations.

The platform was designed to manage workforce allocation, event assignments, attendance workflows, reporting, and operational coordination through a centralized backend system.

The backend used Django with PostgreSQL as the core application layer, with normalized relational modeling to manage employees, attendance hardware, event entities, and operational dependencies.

To support long-running tasks such as report generation, bulk exports, and document processing, the system used Celery with Redis to offload heavy I/O workloads from the request cycle and process them asynchronously.

For deployments, the platform used a Blue-Green deployment pipeline on AWS using GitHub Actions, ECR, CodeDeploy, ECS on EC2, and an Application Load Balancer. This enabled zero-downtime releases for the primary backend and fast rollback through target-group traffic cutover.

The system also included real-time communication using Django Channels and a Redis-backed channel layer to manage WebSocket connections from attendance hardware devices and live operational dashboards.

A major challenge was memory exhaustion during large exports involving reports and binary assets. This was solved by switching to streamed in-memory processing using BytesIO and sequential archive generation, which stabilized worker memory usage during large export jobs.

The platform also enforced role-aware access control and real-time authorization boundaries across operational workflows and administrative interfaces.

title: Professional Background
content: Abhinav Kurup is a backend-focused software engineer with around 2 years of experience building Python-based systems across backend engineering, cloud infrastructure, and applied AI workflows. His work has focused on APIs, real-time systems, retrieval pipelines, automation platforms, and production backend services. He has built systems across collaborative editing, geospatial operations, document intelligence, workforce platforms, and enterprise automation, with 2 backend systems shipped into production and used in live operational environments. His strongest areas are Python backend development, system design, database-heavy workflows, and building scalable backend systems with practical cloud infrastructure. He has worked primarily with Python, Django, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, Redis, AWS, Docker, WebSockets, and LLM-based systems, and also holds an AWS cloud certification. He is primarily targeting backend and AI engineering roles focused on intelligent systems, LLM-powered applications, and production-grade backend architecture.

title: Educational Background
content: Abhinav Kurup completed his Bachelor of Engineering (B.E.) in Computer Engineering from Goa College of Engineering between 2020 and 2024, graduating with a CGPA of 8.55. During his undergraduate studies, he developed strong foundations in computer science, backend engineering, databases, and software development, which later aligned with his focus on scalable backend systems and applied AI workflows. Prior to his engineering degree, he completed his higher secondary education at Kendriya Vidyalaya No. 1 under the CBSE curriculum, securing 94.2% in Class XII between 2018 and 2020.
